title: Capital & Current Account Transactions
content:
Session 2: Fundamentals 
Akshay N. Patel v. Reserve Bank of India [2021 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã AP is the MD of Anzalp Herbal Products Pvt. Ltd., a company engaged inter alia in Merchanting Trade Transactions 
(MTTs) involving pharmaceuticals, herbal products, skincare items, and personal protective equipment (PPE) such as masks, 
gloves, sanitisers, PPE overalls, and ventilators ‚Üí obtained an international MTT contract to act as an intermediary 
between a supplier in China, and a buyer in the United States, for the sale of PPE products. 
‚û¢‚Äã An MTT is a foreign-to-foreign transaction where goods never physically enter India, but the Indian intermediary 
is involved, leading to foreign exchange inflow and outflow through India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã In order to execute this contract, AP wrote on 1 May 2020 to his authorised dealer bank, seeking necessary documentation 
(such as a letter of credit) required to complete the transaction. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On 4 May 2020, the bank informed AP that the RBI had denied permission for the transaction, citing Clause 2(iii) of the 
Revised 2020 MTT Guidelines. 
‚ùñ‚Äã At the relevant time, the Union Ministry of Commerce and Industry and the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) 
had banned the export of PPE products from India, through successive notifications issued under Section 3 of the Foreign 
Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992, amending the Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) 2015‚Äì2020, owing to the 
COVID-19 pandemic. 
‚ùñ‚Äã AP first filed a writ petition before the MPHC, which was unsuccessful, and subsequently approached the SC ‚Üí challenged 
the prohibition as violative of Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Clause 2(iii) of the 2020 MTT Guidelines is unconstitutional for prohibiting MTTs involving PPE products. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the restriction on MTTs violates Articles 14, 19(1)(g), and 21 of the Constitution. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the prohibition satisfies the test of proportionality 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether RBI‚Äôs decision deserves judicial deference, especially in the context of a public health emergency and FEMA 
regulation. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Clause 2(iii) of the 2020 MTT Guidelines: ‚ÄúThe MTT shall be undertaken for the goods that are permitted for 
exports/imports under the prevailing Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) of India as on the date of shipment. All rules, regulations 
and directions applicable to exports (except Export Declaration Form) and imports (except Bill of Entry) shall be complied 
with for the export leg and import leg respectively.‚Äù 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã AP primarily assailed the suitability and breadth of the restriction, arguing that an Indian intermediary in a 
foreign-to-foreign MTT does not affect domestic PPE availability ‚Üí the restriction was overbroad and arbitrary ‚Üí the 
Court answered this through a four-stage proportionality analysis. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Legitimate Aim 
‚û¢‚Äã The objective of ensuring adequate domestic availability of PPE products during COVID-19 was a legitimate and 
compelling State aim. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
PPEs were critical to: protect healthcare workers, safeguard public health, and manage the pandemic ‚Üí 
grounded in: Article 21 (right to life), and Article 47 (public health obligation). 
‚óè‚Äã
AP did not dispute the legitimacy of this aim. 
174 
 
‚û¢‚Äã This objective was of sufficient importance to override economic freedoms under Article 19(1)(g). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Suitability (Rational Nexus) 
‚û¢‚Äã MTTs involve purchase by a resident from a non-resident and resale to another non-resident ‚Üí goods never 
physically enter India, yet are recorded as imports and exports for foreign exchange accounting. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, MTTs are functionally equivalent to traditional imports and exports ‚Üí involve inflow and 
outflow of Indian foreign exchange. 
‚û¢‚Äã Since, FTP banned export of PPE products, and MTTs are analogous to exports/imports, linking MTT 
permissibility to FTP restrictions was held to be suitable. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Allowing MTTs would enable Indian entities to facilitate global diversion and hoarding of PPEs, and 
undermine the export ban. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Necessity (Least Intrusive Effective Measure) 
‚û¢‚Äã REJECTED the argument that mere export ban was sufficient ‚Üí while MTTs do not directly reduce India‚Äôs 
domestic stock, they reduce global availability of PPE, which India might need to procure ‚áí could impair India‚Äôs 
ability to secure PPEs later + contradict the State‚Äôs stance on PPE non-tradability during the pandemic. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A ban on exports alone does not regulate foreign exchange usage ‚Üí allowing MTTs alongside export bans 
would create a policy contradiction, 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence, there was no equally effective but less restrictive alternative ‚Üí a prohibition on MTTs involving PPEs was 
necessary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Balancing / Least Restrictive Means 
‚û¢‚Äã The restriction was general in nature, applied only to goods banned under FTP, and did not impose a blanket ban 
on all MTTs ‚Üí citizens remained free to conduct MTTs in permitted goods. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
HERE, public health and strategic interests outweighed the individual‚Äôs right to act as an intermediary in 
PPE trade during a pandemic. 
‚û¢‚Äã A rational nexus was established between: MTT regulation, forex usage, public health protection. 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, the restriction was not disproportionate. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial Deference & FEMA Context 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court reiterated that where foreign exchange is involved, RBI‚Äôs expertise under FEMA warrants deference ‚Üí 
in a public health emergency, courts may defer even in the absence of precise empirical data. 
‚û¢‚Äã While citing IAMAI v. RBI, the Court clarified that deference is not blind or absolute ‚Üí proportionality review 
remains mandatory. 
‚û¢‚Äã Reference was also made to Gujarat Mazdoor Sabha v. State of Gujarat, where Justice Chandrachud had cautioned 
against uncritical acceptance of emergency claims. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Clause 2(iii) of the 2020 MTT Guidelines is constitutional. 
‚û¢‚Äã The prohibition on MTTs involving PPE products: pursues a legitimate aim, is suitable and necessary, is 
proportionate and balanced, does not violate Articles 14, 19(1)(g), or 21. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court upheld RBI and UOI‚Äôs policy decision, emphasizing that: 
‚û¢‚Äã fundamental rights do not include a right to evade regulation, 
‚û¢‚Äã regulatory frameworks reflect democratic economic control, 
‚û¢‚Äã judicial review persists, but with nuanced deference in expert and emergency contexts. 
 
Riddhisiddhi Bullions Ltd. v. Union of India & Ors. [2020 | BHC | 2 J] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã RS is a major bullion importer, holding Nominated Agency Certificate (NAC) status under India‚Äôs FTP ‚Üí was authorised 
to import gold and had been dealing in very large quantities, importing approximately 16,000 tonnes of gold over the 
preceding five years. 
‚ùñ‚Äã During 2013‚Äì2014, India faced a mini economic crisis, marked by: 
‚û¢‚Äã depreciation of the Indian rupee, 
175 
 
‚û¢‚Äã falling stock markets, 
‚û¢‚Äã continuous depletion of foreign exchange reserves. 
‚ùñ‚Äã A key concern was the rising Current Account Deficit (CAD), largely driven by: 
‚û¢‚Äã continuous increase in gold imports, and 
‚û¢‚Äã corresponding reduction in foreign exchange reserves. 
‚ùñ‚Äã To address this situation, gov + RBI adopted coordinated measures to regulate gold imports, even by licensed importers. 
‚û¢‚Äã One such measure was the 20% export obligation, under which at least 20% of imported gold had to be exported, to 
ensure forex inflow. 
‚û¢‚Äã For Premier Trading Houses, Star Trading Houses, EOUs and SEZ units, imports were permitted exclusively for 
export purposes, not domestic use. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the RBI issued Circular No. 15 dated 22 July 2013, which: 
‚û¢‚Äã required Premier Trading Houses to import gold only for exports, and 
‚û¢‚Äã imposed responsibility on all nominated agencies to ensure compliance with export obligations. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On 22 July 2013, RS dispatched two consignments of gold (100 kg each) from the UAE to India ‚Üí reached India on 23 July 
2013, by which time the RBI Circular was already in force. 
‚ùñ‚Äã RS complied with customs requirements, filed documentation, paid applicable customs duties. 
‚ùñ‚Äã However, authorities held that RS had violated the 100% export obligation imposed by the RBI Circular, and conditions of 
the NAC ‚Üí penalties were imposed, including by the DGFT. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the RBI had the power under FEMA to regulate and restrict gold imports by licensed importers such as 
Riddhisiddhi Bullions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether RBI Circular No. 15 dated 22 July 2013 was applicable to consignments dispatched before its issuance but received 
in India after it came into force. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Circular operated retrospectively and was therefore invalid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the absence of separate Government of India instructions invalidated the RBI Circular. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether penalties imposed by DGFT were lawful. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA, 1999 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 5: CG may, in public interest and in consultation with RBI, impose reasonable restrictions on current 
account transactions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Sections 10(2)‚Äì(5): RBI may: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
authorise persons (Authorised Dealers), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
monitor, regulate and control their foreign exchange dealings, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
issue binding directions to them. 
‚û¢‚Äã Sections 10(4) and 11(1): RBI empowered to issue circulars and directions in consultation with the gov. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 3: CG is empowered to regulate foreign trade by facilitating imports and augmenting exports. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 5: FTP may be formulated and amended by notification. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Customs Act, 1962 
‚û¢‚Äã Complements the 1992 Act. 
‚û¢‚Äã Import/export legality assessed at the point of entry into India and filing of Bill of Entry. 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI Act, 1934 ‚Äì Section 44 referred to, reinforcing RBI‚Äôs overarching role in monetary and forex regulation. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI‚Äôs Power to Regulate Gold Imports 
‚û¢‚Äã Gold imports directly affect foreign exchange reserves and CAD. 
176 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Even though gold is a commodity, its import involves foreign exchange ‚Üí impacts India‚Äôs macro-economic 
stability. 
‚û¢‚Äã The omission of the words ‚Äúgold and bullion‚Äù from certain provisions of FEMA was argued by the petitioners to 
limit RBI‚Äôs power ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
so long as the activity indirectly affects foreign exchange +  concerns conservation and utilisation of forex 
resources ‚Üí RBI‚Äôs regulatory jurisdiction is clearly attracted. 
‚û¢‚Äã The objects of FEMA, like those of FERA, include: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
conservation of foreign exchange, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
orderly development of the forex market, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
facilitation of external trade in national economic interest. 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, RBI, DGFT and the Ministry of Commerce necessarily act in a coordinated regulatory space. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Applicability of the Circular ‚Äì No Retrospectivity 
‚û¢‚Äã Petitioners argued that the shipment was handed over before the Circular was uploaded (7:47 PM UAE time) ‚Üí 
therefore, the Circular should not apply retrospectively ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Import is legally complete only when goods enter India and a Bill of Entry is filed ‚Üí on 23 July 2013, 
when the consignments arrived in India, the RBI Circular was already in force. 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence, there were no retrospective application ‚Üí compliance obligations crystallised on entry into India, not 
dispatch from abroad. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Public Interest & CAD Justification 
‚û¢‚Äã The restrictions were imposed in larger public interest, to arrest deterioration of: foreign exchange reserves, and 
Current Account Deficit. 
‚û¢‚Äã The economic context of 2013‚Äì14 justified: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
tighter control over gold imports, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
even for licensed and high-volume importers. 
‚û¢‚Äã Licences and NACs were held to be conditional, not absolute rights. 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI Circular & Government Instructions 
‚û¢‚Äã Petitioners contended that no separate Government of India instructions were issued to operationalise the Circular 
‚Üí BUT ‚Üí the Circular was issued in consultation with the Gov, as required under FEMA ‚áí no additional 
notification was legally necessary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã DGFT‚Äôs Power to Impose Penalty 
‚û¢‚Äã A conjoint reading of the 1992 Act and the Customs Act empowers DGFT to impose penalties. 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI was exercising its own statutory powers, not encroaching upon DGFT‚Äôs domain. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI has the power to issue Circular No. 15 and regulate gold imports under FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Circular is intra vires FEMA, validly applicable to consignments entering India after its issuance, justified by public 
interest considerations relating to CAD and forex reserves. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The petition was dismissed, and penalties imposed by DGFT were upheld. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court concluded that RBI‚Äôs regulatory action fell squarely within its domain, and deserved judicial deference. 
 
Vishal Tiwari v. Union of India [2023 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The case arose in the aftermath of the Hindenburg Research report alleging serious corporate and market irregularities by 
the Adani Group, leading to sharp fluctuations in share prices and investor concern. 
‚û¢‚Äã The petitioner, Vishal Tiwari, approached the SC under Art.32, pressing his case on two broad fronts: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
First, he sought a direction to: 
‚óè‚Äã
constitute a Special Investigation Team (SIT) to oversee the investigation into the Adani Group, 
and 
177 
 
‚óè‚Äã
ensure that such investigation be court-monitored by the Supreme Court, alleging regulatory 
failure by SEBI. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Second, he sought a direction to SEBI to revoke certain amendments made to: 
‚óè‚Äã
the SEBI (Foreign Portfolio Investors) Regulations, 2014, and 
‚óè‚Äã
the SEBI (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2015 (LODR). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Share price manipulation by the Adani Group 
‚û¢‚Äã Alleged violation of the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Rules (SCRR). 
‚û¢‚Äã FPIs investing heavily in Adani group stocks were alleged to be: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
shell companies located outside India, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
owned by the brother of the Adani Group Chairperson, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
possessing opaque ownership structures. 
‚û¢‚Äã These entities allegedly traded almost exclusively in Adani stocks, artificially inflating prices and creating a 
misleading appearance of financial health and solvency. 
‚û¢‚Äã It was argued that this exposed Indian investors and markets to massive systemic risk. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Short-selling allegations against Hindenburg Research 
‚û¢‚Äã Short selling involves profiting from a decline in stock prices. 
‚û¢‚Äã The petitioner alleged potential market manipulation and regulatory violations. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Supreme Court should direct the constitution of an SIT to oversee and monitor SEBI‚Äôs investigation into the 
Adani Group. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether SEBI‚Äôs amendments to the FPI Regulations, 2014 and LODR Regulations, 2015 should be revoked on grounds of 
regulatory failure. 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the scope of judicial review over SEBI‚Äôs regulatory and policy decisions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether reliance on third-party investigative reports (such as OCCRP) is sufficient to discredit SEBI‚Äôs investigation. 
 
Rule: Minimum Public Shareholding (MPS) Norm: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mandates that at least 25% of a listed company‚Äôs shares must be held by the public. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Introduced via SCRR, 2010. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rationale: 
‚û¢‚Äã India has over 5,000 listed companies but low market depth. 
‚û¢‚Äã High promoter shareholding reduces liquidity. 
‚û¢‚Äã MPS norms aim to improve market depth, liquidity, and reduce promoter dominance. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Challenge to SEBI‚Äôs Regulatory Amendments (FPI & LODR Regulations) 
‚û¢‚Äã The petitioner argued that SEBI had failed in its regulatory mandate by amending disclosure norms. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Original position under FPI Regulations, 2014: 
‚óè‚Äã
Required disclosure of ultimate beneficial ownership by natural persons. 
‚óè‚Äã
Targeted opaque ownership structures. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Disclosure aligned with: 
‚óè‚Äã
Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002, and 
‚óè‚Äã
Rule 9 of the PMLA Maintenance of Records Rules, 2004. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Amendments in 2018 and 2019: 
‚óè‚Äã
Removed the requirement to disclose ownership of FPIs by natural persons. 
‚óè‚Äã
Similar amendments were made to LODR Regulations, and the definition of related party 
transactions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Petitioner‚Äôs argument: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
These amendments diluted transparency. 
178 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enabled shell entities to mask real ownership. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Constituted a regulatory failure, necessitating judicial intervention. 
‚û¢‚Äã Court‚Äôs response: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Regulations are a form of delegated legislation. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
They can be invalidated only if ultra vires the parent statute, or unconstitutional. 
‚óè‚Äã
No such grounds were pleaded or established. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The procedure followed by SEBI showed no illegality or irregularity. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The amendments, in fact, tightened regulatory oversight, rather than weakened it. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Prayer for Constitution of an SIT 
‚û¢‚Äã While it does have the power to transfer investigations to an SIT ‚Üí such power is exercised only when the 
authority demonstrates glaring, willful and deliberate inaction. 
‚û¢‚Äã HERE ‚Üí  SEBI had undertaken 24 investigations into Adani-related allegations. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
22 out of 24 investigations were already completed. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There was no prima facie evidence of bias, mala fides, or regulatory abdication. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reliance on OCCRP report: 
‚û¢‚Äã OCCRP is a third-party investigative journalism organisation. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Petitioners relied solely on inferences from this report and media coverage. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected this reliance because no independent verification was attempted, third-party reports cannot be 
treated as conclusive proof. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial Deference to SEBI 
‚û¢‚Äã Courts are not advisors to expert regulators ‚Üí should not substitute their wisdom for regulatory judgment. 
‚û¢‚Äã There was no apparent regulatory failure attributable to SEBI ‚Üí its expertise, statutory mandate, public-interest 
orientation warranted judicial restraint. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial review over SEBI‚Äôs regulatory and policy framework is limited. Courts may interfere only if the policy violates 
fundamental rights, statutory provisions, constitutional mandates, or is manifestly arbitrary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No grounds were made out to direct SEBI to revoke amendments to the FPI Regulations or LODR Regulations. These 
amendments were validly made in exercise of delegated legislative power. 
‚ùñ‚Äã SEBI has completed 22 out of 24 investigations into the Adani Group. SEBI was directed to complete the remaining two 
investigations expeditiously, preferably within three months. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court did not interfere with the outcome of SEBI‚Äôs investigations and directed SEBI to take them to their logical 
conclusion in accordance with law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No case was made out for transferring the investigation to an SIT. The threshold of extraordinary circumstances was not 
met. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reliance on the OCCRP report and unverified media material was rejected as insufficient to establish regulatory failure. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allegations of conflict of interest against members of the Expert Committee were found to be unsubstantiated and were 
rejected. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Union Government and SEBI were directed to consider the Expert Committee‚Äôs recommendations constructively, as a 
non-exhaustive guide to strengthening the regulatory framework and investor protection. 
‚ùñ‚Äã SEBI and other investigative agencies were directed to probe potential legal violations arising from Hindenburg‚Äôs 
short-selling activities, and to take action if any infraction of law was found.
cases: Patel v. Reserve Bank of India
Vishal Tiwari v. Union of India
